text:
Admission Date:  2004-03-24              Discharge Date:   2018-06-20

Date of Birth:  1983-02-03             Sex:   M

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Penicillins

Attending:Robert
Chief Complaint:
chest pressure and arm pain

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Cardiac Catheterization
Percutaneous Coronary Transluminal Angioplasty with Stenting


History of Present Illness:
51 y/o male with history of HTN and Hypercholesterolemia
presented with new onset of bilateral arm pain associated with
chest tightness. Received four baby aspirin from EMS and taken
to Overlook Hospital. Had full thrombolysis with TKNase as he
would not be able to make it to Fort Valley State University Health System in approproate time frame.
Once thrombolysed, taken to Fort Valley State University Health System via ambulance and upon arival,
still with chest pain so taken emergently to the cath lab where
he had cath and PCI to RCA.

Past Medical History:
GERD
HTN
Hypercholesterolemia
Retinal detachments s/p multiple retinal surgeries

Social History:
Not a smoker. Alcohol use -one drink every couple of weeks. No
drug use.

Family History:
No significant history of familial heart disease. Paternal
Grandmother had MI at age 78.

Physical Exam:
VITALS: T Afebrile HR 55  BP 118/78 RR 15  Sat 98
GENERAL:  well developed male in  NAD
SKIN: no rashes
HEAD: NC/AT
EYES: EOMI, Anicteric, R pupil located laterally on Jonathan, L
pupil dyssymetric
THROAT: No erythema, no tonsillar enlargement, tongue midline
NECK: No stiffness, No masses, No LAD, Palpable carotid pulses,
no bruits, no JVD
CHEST: no supraclavicular or axillary LAD, Lungs Clear to
Asculation, No Wheezes/Rhonchi/Crackles
HEART: normal PMI, RRR, No Murmurs/Gallops/Rubs
BACK: No CVA Tenderness, No spinal tenderness
ABDOMEN: Flat, NABS, Soft, No organomegaly, No masses, No
guarding, No rebound
EXT: No clubbing/cyanosis/edema. Good Pulses.
NEURO: oriented to person, place, time


Pertinent Results:

MCH-32.7* MCHC-34.8 RDW-12.6

EOS-0.4 BASOS-0.2

POTASSIUM-4.3 CHLORIDE-103 TOTAL CO2-21* ANION GAP-18

1977-01-16 01:28AM BLOOD CK(CPK)-515*

1991-03-19 06:10AM BLOOD CK(CPK)-743*
2018-06-20 07:20AM BLOOD CK(CPK)-336*
1977-01-16 01:28AM BLOOD CK-MB-38* MB Indx-7.4* cTropnT-1.54*
1977-01-16 10:30AM BLOOD CK-MB-110* MB Indx-10.8* cTropnT-2.19*
1991-03-19 06:10AM BLOOD CK-MB-33* MB Indx-4.4 cTropnT-1.55*
2018-06-20 07:20AM BLOOD CK-MB-10 MB Indx-3.0 cTropnT-1.39*.
.
Cath:
1. Selective coronary angiography of this right dominant sytem
revealed
single vessel CAD. The RCA was a dominant vessel and was 95%
occluded in
the mid vessel with thrombus. The RCA gave rise to a large PDA.
The LMCA
was without angiographic evidence of flow limiting stenosis. teh
LAD had
no angiographically apparent flow limiting stenosis and gave
rise to a
large D1. The LCX had no angiographically visible flow limiting
lesions
and gave rise to a large OM branch.
2. Limited resting hmeodynamics revealed mildy elevated systemic

pressures.
3. Left ventriculography was deferred.
4. Successful PCI of the proximal and mid RCA with two Cypher
DES
(non-overlapping 3.5 x 18 mm), both post-dilated with a 4.5 mm
balloon.
5. Successful closure of the RFA arteriotomy site with a 6
French
Angioseal device.

FINAL DIAGNOSIS:
1. Angiographic evidence of single vessel coronary artery
disease.
2. Mildly elevated systemic pressures.
3. Successful rescue PCI with stenting of the proximal and
mid-RCA for
failed fibrinolytic therapy for acute inferior myocardial
infarction.
.
Echo:
. Selective coronary angiography of this right dominant sytem
revealed
single vessel CAD. The RCA was a dominant vessel and was 95%
occluded in
the mid vessel with thrombus. The RCA gave rise to a large PDA.
The LMCA
was without angiographic evidence of flow limiting stenosis. teh
LAD had
no angiographically apparent flow limiting stenosis and gave
rise to a
large D1. The LCX had no angiographically visible flow limiting
lesions
and gave rise to a large OM branch.
2. Limited resting hmeodynamics revealed mildy elevated systemic

pressures.
3. Left ventriculography was deferred.
4. Successful PCI of the proximal and mid RCA with two Cypher
DES
(non-overlapping 3.5 x 18 mm), both post-dilated with a 4.5 mm
balloon.
5. Successful closure of the RFA arteriotomy site with a 6
French
Angioseal device.
.
FINAL DIAGNOSIS:
1. Angiographic evidence of single vessel coronary artery
disease.
2. Mildly elevated systemic pressures.
3. Successful rescue PCI with stenting of the proximal and
mid-RCA for
failed fibrinolytic therapy for acute inferior myocardial
infarction.
.
EKG on Admission:
Sinus bradycardia. RSR' pattern in leads V1-V2. ST segment
elevations in
leads II, III and aVF consistent with possible inferior
ischemia/infarction.
.
Follow Up EKG:
Normal sinus rhythm. Right bundle-branch block. Left anterior
fascicular block. Compared to the previous tracing of 1977-01-16
the right bundle-branch block is new. There are also prominent T
wave inversions in leads III and aVF with deep Q waves
suggestive of inferior infarction/ischemia


Brief Hospital Course:
51 yo male transferred from OSH with IMI. Lysed with full dose
TNK, incompletely reperfused and still with pain on arrival at
Fort Valley State University Health System, taken to cath lab, had 95% RCA lesion which was stented
with cyphers x 2. He had an uncomplicated recovery and was
discharged pain free after three days on Toprol Anderson, Sheri,
lisinopril, and plavix.

Medications on Admission:
Atenolol
Niacin
Prilosec

Discharge Medications:
1. Aspirin 325 mg Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) Sig: One (1)
Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) PO DAILY (Daily).

(Daily).

(Daily).

4. Lisinopril 5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).
Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2*
5. Toprol XL 25 mg Tablet Sustained Release 24HR Sig: One (1)
Tablet Sustained Release 24HR PO once a day.
Disp:*30 Tablet Sustained Release 24HR(s)* Refills:*2*


Discharge Disposition:
Home

Discharge Diagnosis:
Coronary Artery Disease
Acute ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction

Discharge Condition:
Stable, without chest pain.

Discharge Instructions:
Please call your doctor or go to the emergency room if you
experience any chest pressure/pain or any symptomes concerning
for a heart attack.

Please make an appointment with your primary care doctor in the
next week.

Please follow up with your cardiologist.

Followup Instructions:
Please follow up with your primary care doctor and cardiologist.

Cardiac rehabilitation.


Completed by:2016-06-10
